blocks.heading: Device Access
blocks.body: São Paulo has undertaken a large-scale purchase of Chromebooks to equip its public school network. **Full-Time Schools (PEI)** aim for a 1:1 device-to-student ratio, while regular schools operate shared **'Chromebook Carts'** on a rotational basis across classrooms.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: Reliable, high-speed internet access remains a critical bottleneck. The dependence on cloud-based platforms (CMSP, Alura, Google Workspace) means that connectivity gaps directly impact instructional delivery, particularly in peripheral and rural school units.

blocks.heading: Internal Tracking
blocks.body: Progress is monitored through **BI Educação**, SEDUC's internal business intelligence dashboard, which tracks platform completion rates — primarily Alura course tracks — as a proxy for instructional engagement.

blocks.heading: Saresp Integration
blocks.body: The annual state exam (**Saresp**) now incorporates questions targeting logical reasoning and digital culture, signaling a formal institutionalization of CS competencies within the state's evaluation framework.

blocks.heading: Artifact Production
blocks.body: The scripted curriculum mandates end-of-bimester project deliverables — for example, designing and presenting a **Scratch game** — as a tangible assessment of computational thinking skills.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No formal public feedback mechanism from students or teachers currently exists. Anonymized student performance data is not publicly available. Reports from social media channels and teacher unions surface recurring concerns regarding limited teacher pedagogical autonomy and the poor maintenance of robotics kits and school Wi-Fi infrastructure.

rows: Computational Thinking

items.label: Full Student & Teacher Handbooks — Se Liga na Educação

blocks.heading: Device Access
blocks.body: Minas Gerais has undertaken a massive procurement of laptops and robotics kits across the state network. Equipment rotation between classrooms is managed predominantly through **mobile charging carts**, enabling shared use across school units.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: Connectivity and Wi-Fi stability remain primary bottlenecks, particularly for simultaneous access to AI platforms, which demand consistent high-bandwidth environments rarely available in standard classroom settings.

blocks.heading: Internal Tracking
blocks.body: Progress is monitored through **Diagnostic and Quarterly Assessments** focused on digital literacy competencies, providing a structured measure of student engagement with the curriculum.

blocks.heading: Practical Work
blocks.body: Bi-monthly **'Hands-on' (Mão na Massa)** projects are a core assessment mechanism, ranging from AI prototypes to robotics circuits, requiring students to apply computational thinking in tangible deliverables.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: Performance microdata and implementation efficacy results for 2026 are not yet publicly disclosed. Teacher feedback highlights the need for better technical support and the challenge of delivering a dense curriculum — including AI concepts — within a single 45-minute weekly class.

glance.label: Tech & Innovation Teachers (delivered by Math teachers)

blocks.heading: Transversal Integration — No Standalone Subject
blocks.body: Computational Thinking is not a standalone subject. It is embedded as a transversal competency within the Mathematics and its Technologies curriculum, delivered entirely by Mathematics teachers.

Concept maps, infographics, hypertexts, and curation tools

blocks.heading: Device Access
blocks.body: Massive distribution of Chromebooks for 100% of High School and EJA students, including data packages for off-campus use. Charging cabinets are used for organization, storage, and rotation of equipment between classrooms.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: Ongoing implementation of high-performance Wi-Fi to support simultaneous access across classrooms. Infrastructure rollout is still in progress across the state network.

blocks.heading: Escola do Futuro Program
blocks.body: Focused investment in robotics and innovation labs in certified units. Currently active in 19 schools, with a goal of reaching 75 schools by the end of 2026.

blocks.heading: Monitoring & Diagnostics
blocks.body: Progress is tracked via CAEd with detailed student-level data (restricted) and consolidated public indicators via the BI Panel. The system uses integrated assessments within Mathematics to measure Computational Thinking skills, with SIGAE providing pedagogical feedback to teachers.

blocks.body: In High School, 'Digital Culture' and 'Computational Thinking' are optional CCEs chosen by students among 25 available electives. All EF and HS students will have the three BNCC Computing axes integrated transversally from 2026 via the Digital Education Curriculum.

blocks.heading: Estudantes Conectados Program (2025, R$ 80M)
blocks.body: Distribution of **18,000 chromebooks** for teachers, cloudbooks and notebooks for students with charging carts in all 1,046 schools; interactive screens for all classrooms. Target: **100% of schools with high-performance Wi-Fi by July 2026**. In 2026, 107 NTE coordinators were specifically trained for BNCC Computing.

blocks.heading: Maker Laboratories
blocks.body: Target of up to **120 Maker Labs** across the state network (expansion ongoing in 2025–2026). Each lab offers space for prototyping, digital fabrication, and robotics.

blocks.heading: NTEs (Educational Technology Centers)
blocks.body: Each Regional Education Coordination has an NTE responsible for teacher training in pedagogical use of equipment and for implementing BNCC Computing. **107 coordinators** are the main implementation vehicle.

blocks.heading: CaTec+ (Technical Education)
blocks.body: ~**30,000 students** enrolled in technical courses in CEDUPs, with more robust technology infrastructure for applied computing.

blocks.heading: SEAESC (2025)
blocks.body: State assessment system launched in 2025, with first application in November 2025 for **128,000 students**. Assesses Portuguese and Mathematics. Results will be public by school and will classify schools into three groups for the **Prêmio Reconhecer** (awards up to R$ 1.2 million). No specific digital competency assessment.

blocks.heading: CCE Assessment
blocks.body: Assessment of the 'Digital Culture' and 'Computational Thinking' electives is process-based and project-driven, per SED pedagogical scripts. No standardized instrument or specific public result exists.

blocks.heading: Institutional Evaluation
blocks.body: Annual evaluation in all 1,046 state schools involving students, parents, teachers, and staff, covering four axes (Pedagogical Policies, Organizational Climate, Infrastructure, Management). Does not include specific digital competency assessment.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No public feedback channel exists for students or teachers on computing components. Nota Técnica CEE/SC 001/2024 explicitly acknowledges that 'digital education as a curricular component will be disciplinary or transversal, at the choice of the school network' — signaling the state is still defining the final model.

blocks.heading: No Standalone Computing Subject
blocks.body: Computing is not a standalone subject. The axes below are implemented transversally across all disciplines (EF and HS), with practical deepening via Ciseb immersions. The 2025 Computing Curriculum Reference maps these axes to the DCE-PA.

items.label: New High School — Normative Acts (PAIEs, CEE/PA Resolutions)

blocks.heading: Conecta Educação Program (2025)
blocks.body: Distribution of **36,000 Chromebooks** with mobile carts to 920 state schools (36 per school), with an investment of over R$ 64 million. An additional **17,500 Chromebooks** distributed to teachers.

blocks.heading: Internet & Connectivity
blocks.body: **1,649 Starlink antennas** installed in state schools, including rural, indigenous, and quilombola annexes under the Connectivity Law (nº 14.172). Covers 100% of the 998 state schools and their annexes. Urban schools also have fiber optic access (ConectaNet/WebEscola).

blocks.heading: Ciseb (Innovation and Sustainability Center)
blocks.body: Currently **4 units in operation** (Belém and Santo Antônio do Tauá), with six immersion rooms covering: digital prototyping and fabrication, maker culture, digital culture, VR/AR, robotics, and creative computing & AI. Any school in Pará can schedule visits for groups of up to **120 students**.

blocks.heading: Device Model
blocks.body: Chromebooks in mobile carts with rotating classroom use. Integral schools (**162 units, ~48,000 students** in 2025) have more systematic device access. Despite universal Starlink coverage, connection stability in riverine, indigenous, and remote areas remains a bottleneck for continuous cloud platform use.

items.label: SisPAE — Public Results Portal
items.note: Biannual census assessment in Portuguese and Mathematics. Results public by school, DRE, and municipality.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: Pará participates in the national SAEB. In 2023, the state rose from **26th to 6th place** in the national Ideb ranking, an advance of 1.3 points. General results are public via INEP.

blocks.heading: Bot Ciseb
blocks.body: A generative AI tool built on the Computing Curriculum Reference and DCE-PA, made available to network teachers to support transversal computing lesson planning. Signals the beginning of a pedagogical support ecosystem, but no public usage data has been released.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No specific assessment instrument exists for the three BNCC Computing axes. No public feedback channel exists for students or teachers. A DRE Belém 4 technology coordinator publicly stated that without the Feb/2026 Ciseb training, she would feel unprepared to implement the reference — signaling that teacher readiness outside the capital and DRE technology hubs is still incipient.

blocks.heading: No Standalone Computing Subject
blocks.body: Computing is not a standalone subject. The axes below are delivered transversally within regular disciplines (EF and HS) and more structured in robotics-enabled schools. In HS, students who choose technology tracks in the Formative Itineraries deepen the first two axes.

LEGO Education kits (SIMROBÓTICA/SIM Inova), Arduino, block programming platforms, Scratch.

blocks.heading: Chromebooks & Smart TVs
blocks.body: Mass distribution underway — all classrooms in the state network are receiving **60" Smart TVs** and Chromebooks for student use, integrated with the Structured Teaching System and Plurall platform. The goal is 1:1 coverage, currently in gradual expansion.

blocks.heading: Educational Robotics (SIMROBÓTICA)
blocks.body: Over **204 state schools** have robotics kits (LEGO Education/SIM Inova), with continuous expansion. Each participating teacher receives SIM Inova certification and integrated continuing education. Covers both Middle School and High School.

blocks.heading: Maker Spaces & Labs
blocks.body: Target of **30 schools with Maker Spaces** and 30 with dedicated robotics labs, as part of a R$ 214 million investment in the New High School program.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: High-speed internet is a declared priority of the EducAção 10 Anos program. New and renovated schools receive full Wi-Fi coverage. In rural areas connectivity remains variable, especially in rural and indigenous schools.

blocks.heading: Structured System (FGV)
blocks.body: Printed and digital bimonthly workbooks for all students in EF Finals, HS, and EJA (628 schools, ~320k students), with R$ 110 million investment in 2026. Content is regionalized and aligned with DRC-MT and BNCC.

blocks.heading: FGV Bimonthly Assessments
blocks.body: Diagnostic and process assessments applied bimonthly across the network, managed by FGV. Results are available in real time to managers and teachers via the platform. Computing and robotics are assessed as part of regular disciplines — no specific public instrument exists.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: MT participates in SAEB every two years. In 2023 the state rose from **22nd to 8th place** in the national High School ranking. General results are public; school-level microdata accessible via INEP.

blocks.heading: STEAM Science Fair
blocks.body: The annual **Mostra Científica STEAM SEDUC-MT** (since 2020) is the main showcase for technology and computing projects, with regional and state-level phases. In 2024, 61 projects were selected; in 2025, results were shared via TikTok (#steammt2025).

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No public feedback channel exists for students or teachers on computing/robotics classes. Academic research (Casagrande, Lima & Maciel, 2025) identified a tension between strong digital platform assessment and weak day-to-day pedagogical integration. Teachers report the approach is stronger in digital evaluation than in everyday classroom practice.

blocks.heading: Two-tier model
blocks.body: In High School, the four axes below are covered by the mandatory 'Tecnologia e Inovação' UC for all students. In Middle School, the last two axes are delivered transversally within Mathematics and Languages — no standalone subject exists.

rows: Creative Processes & Digital Citizenship
Propose and test ethical, innovative solutions for real problems, integrating digital technologies (EMIF04PE).
Project-based learning; ethical and misuse case analysis; digital prototype production.
Google Workspace, digital prototyping tools.

Model algorithms applied to real problems; understand automation, IoT, and AI in local context.
Programming and modeling challenges; algorithmic efficiency analysis; Python projects.
Scratch, Python, network simulators.

Understand network architecture, cloud computing, and digital security.
Hands-on hardware and networking exploration; simulations; security and privacy debates.
Circuit and network simulators.

Critically analyze the social impact of technology; combat disinformation; understand LGPD and digital footprint.
Media auditing; digital footprint mapping; AI and fake news debates; critical content production.
Social media, fact-checking tools, audio/video editors.

blocks.heading: Device Access
blocks.body: Primary delivery relies on **rotating computer lab access** in regular schools (30h/week). Full-time EREs and ETEs (45h/week) use computers more systematically. Unlike SP and ES, the state has not conducted a mass device distribution to students.

blocks.heading: PE+Digital Program (2024–2025)
blocks.body: Distribution of notebooks to over **13,000 permanent staff** (teachers and school managers) with a R$ 44 million investment, focused on pedagogical planning via SIEPE.

blocks.heading: Compet Médio-Tec (SEE + SECTI + FACEPE)
blocks.body: Annual program funding projects in **AI, IoT, robotics, and cloud computing** in schools, with scholarships for High School and ETE students.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: **89.8%** of PE public schools have internet access (Basic Education Yearbook 2024), but only **21% have an active computer lab**. Wi-Fi is unstable especially in inland areas (Agreste and Sertão). The 2025 Digital Culture Guide explicitly includes unplugged activities as a compensatory strategy for schools with limited device access.

items.label: SAEPE / IDEPE Portal
items.note: Annual census-based assessment covering Portuguese and Mathematics. Aggregate results are public; microdata restricted to managers.

items.label: FACEPE — Compet Médio-Tec
items.note: Annual showcase and award for technology projects by HS and ETE students.

blocks.heading: Project-Based Assessment
blocks.body: The UC Tecnologia e Inovação teacher guide specifies assessment primarily through **creative artifacts and prototypes** — apps, local problem solutions, and multimedia productions. There is no standardized exam specific to the UC.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No public feedback channel exists for students or teachers on the Tecnologia e Inovação UC. Fundação Telefônica Vivo (2026) found that only ~30% of PE teachers can integrate digital competencies in a qualified way. Teachers report difficulties with variable infrastructure across schools and the density of content within limited weekly periods.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop abstraction processes and algorithms for real-world problem-solving.
Experimental Practices (Stations) applied to the territory.
Scratch, Block Languages, and Unplugged Logic.
Body Culture & Tech
Integrate technological languages into human and bodily expressions.
Media production and critical analysis of tech impact on society.
Video/Audio editing, Audio, and Social Media.
Innovation & Territoriality
Create tech solutions that respect and empower local contexts.
Social Intervention and Scientific Initiation projects.
Google Workspace, Canva, and SABE Platform.

blocks.heading: Digital Sovereignty
blocks.body: Focus on building national data infrastructure to reduce dependency on Big Tech. The state actively promotes the use of open standards and national platforms alongside Google Workspace tools.

blocks.heading: Mobile Devices & Guidelines
blocks.body: Strict guidelines under **Law 15.100/2025** and **Decree 12.385/2025** govern the pedagogical and responsible use of mobile devices in schools, balancing access with focused learning.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: Tablets are provided with data chips to guarantee access to Google Workspace and AI assistants off-campus. The **Conectar Program** aims to deliver high-speed Wi-Fi and fiber optics to all school units.

blocks.heading: AI Assistants
blocks.body: Tools like **Tutor IA / PIÁ** and **Acompanhatec** are integrated to support pedagogical tracking and personalized learning pathways for students.

blocks.heading: SABE System
blocks.body: Process-based evaluation integrated into Mathematics and Language exams, tracking Computational Thinking competencies within the existing assessment framework.

blocks.heading: Data Management
blocks.body: Assessment results are discussed in **Pedagogical Journeys (Jornadas Pedagógicas)** for structured intervention planning, connecting data directly to classroom practice.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No public feedback channel exists. Teacher feedback is collected exclusively via annual internal surveys conducted by SEC-BA, with results not publicly disclosed.

blocks.heading: No Standalone Computing Subject
blocks.body: Amazonas has not published a state computing curriculum complement. The components below reflect what is anticipated by the national BNCC Computação and the isolated initiatives identified in the network — without formal state curricular systematization.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, and problem-solving; introduction to programming language for teachers and, indirectly, for students.
Programming course for teachers (2024, via SEDUC); Digital After-School activities via Cemeam; unplugged approach in schools without connectivity.
Plataforma Educação (Moodle), Saber Mais, offline DVDs for areas without internet.
Digital World
Understand how networks, devices, and the internet work; develop skills for the pedagogical use of available technologies.
Exploration via Saber Mais and Cemeam (video lessons and educational games); digital resource use in connected urban schools.
Saber Mais (sabermais.am.gov.br), Cemeam video lessons, Portal Educacional.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critical and responsible use of technology; introduction to digital citizenship and data protection.
Integrated into regular disciplines in connected schools; no specific systematization across the network.
Plataforma Educação, Cemeam digital resources, Google Workspace in schools with access.

items.note: Approved by CEE/AM. No specific computing section.

items.label: SEDUC-AM — Referencial Curricular Amazonense (RCA-EF, 2020)
items.note: Foundational document for EF aligned with BNCC.

items.label: Saber Mais
items.note: Multimedia portal categorized by subject and grade (partnership with Escola Digital / Instituto Inspirare).

items.label: Plataforma Educação (Moodle)
items.note: Remote/hybrid teaching platform with video lessons and digital notebooks. Developed during the pandemic for areas with limited connectivity.

items.label: Aula em Casa
items.note: Video lessons, worksheets, and digital notebooks — with offline DVD version for areas without internet.

items.label: Portal Educacional
items.note: School management system and student document access.

blocks.heading: Connectivity: The State's Largest Barrier
blocks.body: Only 64.2% of public schools in Amazonas have internet access (Censo Escolar 2025), vs. 93.1% nationally. In rural areas the rate drops to 52.5%; in indigenous schools to 46.3%. Amazonas is among the states with the worst connection quality (~0.1 Mbps per student).

blocks.heading: Federal Connectivity Program
blocks.body: The federal government plans to connect 2,000+ more schools in Amazonas by end of 2026, expanding coverage from 1,309 (25.4%) to a broader reach. In 2025 alone, connected schools grew from 17.2% to 25.4% in 7 months.

blocks.heading: Offline Delivery Model
blocks.body: Amazonas maintains **DVD distribution** of pedagogical content for schools in low or no-connectivity areas — a strategy unique in Brazil reflecting the state's geographic reality. Printed study guides are distributed during river drought periods.

blocks.heading: Cemeam
blocks.body: The **Centro de Mídias de Educação do Amazonas** produces and distributes video lessons and digital content for the Digital After-School program, serving students from Grade 5 to High School via television and internet.

blocks.heading: Devices
blocks.body: The state network has not made a large-scale device purchase comparable to SP, ES, or PA. Urban schools in Manaus have computer labs; rural schools frequently lack access to devices.

blocks.heading: SADEAM
blocks.body: Annual assessment applied since 2008, covering Portuguese and Mathematics — predominantly for Grade 2 EF. In 2025, 50,260 students participated (86% of the assessed cohort), with an 8% advance in literacy. Results are shared with focal points and municipal secretaries. **Digital competencies are not assessed.**

blocks.heading: AVAM
blocks.body: Complementary diagnostic assessment; in 2024, evaluated 90,000 state network students. Results used internally for pedagogical planning.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: Amazonas participates in the national SAEB. In 2025, more than 1,800 schools were included. The state achieved score 5.9 in Ideb 2023 for Early Childhood state network — above the federal target of 5.4.

blocks.heading: No Computing Assessment
blocks.body: No assessment instrument exists for computing competencies. There is no rubric, grade, or result related to the three BNCC Computação axes. Teachers report that technology training is insufficient and sporadic, and no public feedback channel on computing or digital education exists.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: In High School, robotics and programming are offered as optional **Unidades Curriculares Eletivas** within the Itinerários Formativos. In Middle School, BNCC Computação axes are being incorporated transversally into regular disciplines. Computational thinking, gamification, and digital culture have been part of state teacher training since 2017.

rows: Computational Thinking & Robotics
Develop logical reasoning, programming, and problem-solving; assemble and program robots in the context of Natural Sciences and Mathematics.
Weekly Robotics Elective (HS): assembly of motorized models, programming, and control. Web Programming and Game Design elective in pioneer schools.
Robotics kits (2,380 kits for 238 HS schools), Chromebooks, Google Workspace.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how devices, networks, and digital tools work; develop authorship and digital production skills.
Pedagogical use of Chromebooks and Google Workspace; Escola Digital platform for content curation; Canva for Education for creative production.
Chromebooks (28,000 units), Google Workspace (97,000 licences), Canva for Education, Escola Digital.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Develop digital citizenship, critical and ethical use of technology; counter misinformation and understand the social impact of AI.
Teacher training focused on gamification and digital culture (since 2017); integration into regular disciplines; thematic electives.
Google Workspace, Escola Digital, Moodle (Profe Formação).

items.note: Curricular matrices and pedagogical guidelines by stage.

blocks.heading: Profe Digital (since 2023, R$ 49 million)
blocks.body: Distribution of **28,000 Chromebooks** to 366 schools (120,000+ students), **14,000 notebooks** for teachers, 97,000+ Google Workspace for Education licences, and a partnership with Canva for Education. Target of 50,000 machines by 2027 (including Espaços Makers).

blocks.heading: Robótica em Ação (Jun/2025)
blocks.body: Delivery of **2,380 robotics kits** to 238 high schools, with in-person training for Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and Biology teachers. Robotics electives launched in **August 2025**, once a week, 2-hour sessions.

blocks.heading: Wi-Fi nas Escolas
blocks.body: High-speed internet (Access Points with up to 1,024 simultaneous connections) deployed in **100% of urban schools** (346 units), benefiting 116,000 students. Expansion to rural schools planned for 2025/2026.

blocks.heading: Connectivity: Above National Average
blocks.body: 89.1% of state schools have internet access (Censo Escolar 2025/ENEC) — well above the regional average (60.5%) and national average (69.7%). 100% of schools have adequate energy supply; 84.8% have adequate speed; 82.5% have compatible Wi-Fi.

blocks.heading: Mestrado Profissional em Governança e Transformação Digital
blocks.body: Partnership with the **Universidade Federal do Tocantins (UFT)** for postgraduate education of state network teachers, focused on digital governance and computational modeling.

blocks.heading: SAETO
blocks.body: Created by Law nº 4,395/2024, covering Portuguese and Mathematics for Grades 2 and 5 EF (municipal networks) and Grades 5/9 EF and HS Year 3 (state network). Results influence the distribution of the Educational ICMS to municipalities (up to R$ 100 million). In 2025, Tocantins achieved **61% performance**, exceeding MEC's 55% target. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEB 2025
blocks.body: Tocantins recorded a historic **95% participation rate** among High School students. In Ideb 2023, the state achieved an overall average of 4.9, ranking **1st in the North Region**.

blocks.heading: Prêmio Ideb Mais
blocks.body: Annual award of R$ 23.7 million (2025) for schools achieving a minimum 0.3% Ideb growth, with bonuses of R$ 750–R$ 6,000 per staff member. Functions as a performance incentive tied to external assessments.

blocks.heading: No Standardized Computing Assessment
blocks.body: No public standardized instrument exists for computing competencies. Robotics electives are assessed procedurally by teachers, focused on practical assembly and programming projects. The Fundação Telefônica Vivo (2022) noted that advances have been achieved 'mainly through permanent dialogue with teachers and a lot of planning' — without formal curricular systematization yet.

items.label: Resultados no Diário Oficial do Tocantins

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: In High School, **'Tecnologia e Inovação'** is a semester elective in the Catálogo de Eletivas, explicitly structured around the three BNCC Computação axes. In full-time schools (Cepis) and military colleges (CEPMGs), robotics is a regular elective. Across both EF and EM, the three axes are being incorporated transversally into regular disciplines from 2026.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, programming logic, and prototyping; assemble and program robots applying Natural Sciences and Mathematics concepts.
Elective 'Tecnologia e Inovação' in HS; Educational robotics in Cepis and military colleges with assembly kits and programming software.
Robotics kits, Chromebooks, Google Workspace for Education, Scratch.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how devices, networks, and digital tools work; develop authorship and digital production competencies.
Pedagogical use of Chromebooks and Google Workspace; NetEscola portal for digital activities; teacher training via Google for Education.
Chromebooks (135,000+ distributed), Google Workspace, Portal NetEscola, NetEscola app.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze the impact of technologies; develop digital citizenship, ethical and safe use of media; understand social implications of AI.
Contemporary Cross-Cutting Themes integrated into the curriculum; thematic electives; discussions in regular disciplines.
Google Workspace, curation tools, CAEd platform for assessment.

items.label: DC-GO — Documento Curricular para Goiás (EF + DC-GO Ampliado)
items.note: Includes digital competencies and 3-volume Ampliado with computing reference matrix.

items.label: DC-GOEM — Documento Curricular para Goiás — Ensino Médio
items.note: HS base curriculum organized by knowledge area and formative itineraries.

items.label: Catálogo de Eletivas 2025 (includes Tecnologia e Inovação)
items.note: Semester elective portfolio with pedagogical scripts aligned to BNCC Computação axes (programming, logic, digital culture).

items.label: Matriz Curricular 2025 — Caderno Orientador
items.note: Full curricular organization for EF and EM in 2025.

items.label: Portal NetEscola
items.note: SEDUC-GO platform with online content and activities for all stages.

items.label: REVISA GOIÁS — Material Didático
items.note: Bimonthly teaching material for Portuguese and Mathematics (Grade 6 to HS), digitized for teacher use.

items.label: Orientações para implementação do DC-GO Ampliado — Computação (Goiânia, 2026)
items.note: Detailed document with skills matrix by year (EI to HS), plugged/unplugged activities, and transversal guidelines — model for the entire state network.

blocks.heading: 100% Connectivity Achieved (2025)
blocks.body: Goiás universalized high-speed internet across all 1,014 state schools — a target recorded by Censo Escolar 2024, placing the state among the first in the country. The Conectar program invested R$ 4.8 million in network infrastructure.

blocks.heading: Chromebooks — 135,000+ Distributed
blocks.body: Progressive distribution since 2021 to Grade 9 EF, and HS Years 2 and 3, with accumulated investment of R$ 329 million. Students keep the device for home use during the school year. In 2025, **60,000 new Chromebooks** were delivered to HS Year 2.

blocks.heading: Google Workspace Partnership
blocks.body: Goiás received a technical visit from Google in 2024 as recognition for best practices, with ongoing teacher training via Google for Education.

blocks.heading: Robótica Educacional — R$ 58 Million since 2022
blocks.body: Kits delivered to **173+ school units**, including all 74 **CEPMGs** (Military State Colleges, focused on Grades 6–7 EF) and Cepis, with annual teacher training. The 2026 training cycle launched in February 2026.

blocks.heading: Programa Jornada Ampliada e Cepis
blocks.body: Full-time schools offer 4 elective periods per week in EF and 2 in HS. Robotics is one of the available electives, with kits, tablets, and supplementary materials.

blocks.heading: SAEGO
blocks.body: Annual 3-stage assessment (Diagnostic, Formative, Institutional) for Grades 5 and 9 EF and HS Year 3, covering Portuguese and Mathematics. Created in 2011, managed by CAEd/UFJF, aligned with SAEB. Historic ~92% participation in 2023. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEGO-Alfa
blocks.body: Reading fluency assessment for Grades 2 and 5 EF (state and municipal networks), linked to the Educational ICMS and the AlfaMais Goiás program.

blocks.heading: SAEB 2025
blocks.body: In Ideb 2025, the state network achieved **4.8 in High School — the best national result for a state HS network**. Goiás participated with high engagement.

blocks.heading: No Standardized Computing Assessment
blocks.body: No public standardized instrument exists for computing competencies. Electives are assessed procedurally by teachers, focused on practical projects. The Goiânia 2026 document recommends assessment integrated into regular disciplines — no separate computing grade. The 100% salary bonus for all 42,000 staff creates a structural incentive focused on Portuguese and Mathematics, not computing.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: **'Fundamentos de Computação'** is mandatory in Grade 5 and Grade 9 of elementary school and in all High School series (Res. CEEd 379/2024). In the Early Years of EF, it can be taught as a component, project, or transversally. Res. 384/2025 incorporates BNCC Computação into the High School Formação Geral Básica. The RCG Computação (2024) maps skills for all three axes across the entire Basic Education cycle.

rows: Computational Thinking

Develop logical reasoning, problem decomposition, algorithms, and programming logic; understand prototyping and complex problem-solving; ensure basic digital literacy (read, write, calculate, and code).
Plugged and unplugged activities; standalone curricular component in Grade 5, Grade 9 EF, and HS; HackaTchê Robotics challenge for EF Final Years.
Robotics kits (HackaTchê Robotics), Scratch, block-based programming tools; unplugged activities.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how devices, networks, the internet, cloud computing, and AI work; develop authorship and digital production skills.
Practical exploration with available devices; integration with regular disciplines; pedagogical use of school digital tools.
Google Workspace (via schools), computer labs, Escola.RS app for pedagogical records.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze the impact of technologies; develop digital citizenship, ethical use of media, and online safety; combat misinformation.
HackaTchê Business (HS): development of technology solutions for SDGs; discussions integrated into regular disciplines.
Digital creation tools, presentation platforms, SEDUC-RS Education Portal.

items.label: Referencial Curricular Gaúcho (RCG) — Interactive Platform
items.note: Online portal with RCG EF (2018) and EM (2021) by area and component.

items.label: RCG — Downloads por caderno (EF e EM) — UNDIME/RS
items.note: Notebooks by knowledge area accessible via UNDIME/RS.

items.label: Resolução CEEd nº 379/2024 — Computação obrigatória na Educação Básica
items.note: Complete normative act: mandatory grades, teacher profile, and 4-year transitional period.

items.label: Resolução CEEd nº 384/2025 — Novo Ensino Médio RS (BNCC Computação à FGB)
Matriz de Referência 2025 (includes computing transversal guidelines)
Matrizes Curriculares 2025 — Ensino Regular (includes Tecnologia e Robótica / Inovação for EMTI)
HackaTchê — Business (HS) & Robotics (EF Final Years)
items.note: Annual innovation competition in its 4th (Business) and 2nd (Robotics) editions in 2025.

blocks.heading: 2024 Flood Disaster: 700+ Schools Affected
blocks.body: The April–May 2024 floods — the worst climate disaster in RS history — affected more than 700 state schools, destroying computer labs, equipment, and furniture. The state invested R$ 83 million via Programa Agiliza Educação for reconstruction, but the impact on technology infrastructure is still felt in 2025.

blocks.heading: Programa Agiliza Educação
blocks.body: Direct resource transfer to schools (R$ 90k–R$ 140k per unit) for autonomous use in renovations, equipment, and teaching materials — including computer lab equipment. Created in 2021 and intensified after the floods.

blocks.heading: Ensino Médio em Tempo Integral (EMTI)
blocks.body: 205 EMTI schools in 2024, expanding to **295 in 2025** (target: 50% of HS schools by 2026). EMTI curricular matrices include **'Tecnologia e Robótica'** and **'Tecnologia e Inovação'** as components, taught by Mathematics/Natural Sciences teachers.

blocks.heading: Connectivity & Devices
blocks.body: No mass individual device program comparable to GO or ES. State schools have access to computer labs and, in some cases, Chromebooks via federal programs. The Programa Escolas Resilientes includes climate-resilient infrastructure plans.

blocks.heading: HackaTchê Robotics
blocks.body: Annual competition delivering robotics kits to the **five finalist teams** from EF Final Years. The challenge structure requires schools to already have robotics development as a prerequisite for registration.

blocks.heading: SAERS
blocks.body: Annual census-based assessment for Grade 2, 5, and 9 EF and HS Year 3, covering Portuguese and Mathematics across the entire state and municipal network. Established by decree in 2022, with results linked to the Educational ICMS for municipalities. In 2023, evaluated 333,337 students. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: Simulado SAEB 2025
blocks.body: Applied September 8–18, 2025 across all state schools, with cash prizes for top-performing students by class. Preparation tool for the national SAEB.

blocks.heading: Avaliação Diagnóstica Trimestral
blocks.body: Quarterly assessments with grades (Grade 3 to Grade 9 EF and HS) or Descriptive Report (Grade 1–2 EF), recorded in the Escola.RS app. Computing has no specific separate assessment — it is integrated into the curricular component according to each school's criteria.

blocks.heading: HackaTchê as Project Assessment
blocks.body: HackaTchê Business (HS) and Robotics (EF Final Years) serve as the main project assessment showcase. In 2025, both competitions included expert jury evaluation and awards. The competition is voluntary and does not replace formal curricular assessment.

blocks.heading: Teacher Gap Acknowledged
blocks.body: The state explicitly recognized in Res. 379/2024 that the 4-year transitional period exists because 'there are not enough qualified Computing teachers.' The 2024 floods introduced 'climate justice and climate education' as central principles in the 2025 Reference Matrix, reflecting structural impact on the school community.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: In regular EM and EEMTIs, computing is being incorporated transversally from 2026 via the DCRC Complement (pending homologation). In **EEEPs** (131 vocational schools), computing is taught in depth through technical courses integrated into High School. The three axes below reflect both what is anticipated in the DCRC Complement and what is already practiced in EEEPs and EEMTI electives.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, programming, and problem-solving; in EEEPs, deepen systems development, networking, and computer graphics in a professional context.
EEEPs: practical IT labs with technical projects and paid internship (6,000 partner companies); EEMTIs: thematic electives; regular EM: transversal integration from 2026.
Tablets (228,000 distributed in 2024), EEEP computer labs, Google Workspace, Scratch.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how networks, operating systems, cloud computing, and digital security work; develop practical IT skills for the job market (EEEPs).
Curso Técnico em Redes de Computadores (EEEPs): hands-on lab practice; pedagogical use of tablets and devices in EEMTIs and regular EM.
Tablets, network equipment (EEEP labs), Google Workspace, digital platforms.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze the social impact of technologies; develop digital citizenship and responsible, ethical media use in the Ceará context.
Integrated into regular disciplines and Projeto de Vida in EEMTIs; discussions on guided pedagogical cell phone use (Ceará Educa Mais); thematic electives.
Tablets, cell phones with guided pedagogical use, Google Workspace.

items.note: Base document for the entire network, aligned with BNCC. No explicit computing skills — Complement in development.

items.note: Curricular organization for EF and EM for 2026, including BNCC Computação guidelines.

items.label: Proposta do Complemento ao DCRC — Computação (public consultation Dec/2025–Jan/2026)
items.note: Document with computing skills for EI and EF. Not yet homologated.

items.label: Diretrizes para o Ano Letivo 2025
items.note: Annual pedagogical guidelines with digital education as a key axis of Ceará Educa Mais.

items.label: SPAECE — Resultados históricos (1992–2025)
items.note: Results by school, Crede, municipality, and state in historical series.

blocks.heading: 228,000 Tablets Delivered (2024, R$ 100 million)
blocks.body: In 2024, the government delivered 228,000 tablets to HS Year 1 students across the state network. The goal is for all state public school students to have a tablet. In 2025, an additional 6,034 tablets were delivered to HS Year 1. Devices are for integrated pedagogical use, not just content delivery.

blocks.heading: EEEPs — 131 Vocational Schools
blocks.body: Specialized laboratories for technical courses in **Informática**, **Redes de Computadores**, **Desenvolvimento de Sistemas**, and **Computação Gráfica**. In 2023, R$ 1.6 million was invested in revitalizing labs across all 131 technical courses. Mandatory paid internship in Year 3 with **6,000 partner companies**.

blocks.heading: EEMTIs — 382 Full-Time Schools
blocks.body: 7–9h daily schedule with Formative Itineraries, Projeto de Vida, and Electives — including technology electives. Each EEMTI defines its own electives with pedagogical autonomy.

blocks.heading: Guided Cell Phone Use (Ceará Educa Mais)
blocks.body: Ceará chose to guide the pedagogical use of personal cell phones in schools as an access-expansion strategy, rather than banning them — regulated by each school according to its charter.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: The state has not published specific Wi-Fi coverage data for the state network, but EEEPs have quality internet for technical labs. In EEMTIs and regular schools, connectivity is variable.

blocks.heading: SPAECE: Brazil's Longest-Running State Assessment (since 1992)
blocks.body: Evaluates Portuguese and Mathematics in Grade 2, 5, and 9 EF (state and municipal networks via PAIC) and HS Years 2 and 3 (census-based). Historical public results by school, Crede, and municipality. The Prêmio Escola Nota 10 is linked to SPAECE-Alfa. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SPAECE EM 2025
blocks.body: Applied in October 2025 for HS Years 2 and 3, with preliminary results released in December 2025. High network mobilization: 100% of HS completers enrolled in ENEM 2024 (97,283 students).

blocks.heading: Ceará Científico
blocks.body: Annual science fair promoted by SEDUC, with regional and state stages. In 2025, **259 projects from 187 schools** participated in four categories, including technology projects. Functions as a showcase for interdisciplinary projects with a technological component.

blocks.heading: EEEP Assessment
blocks.body: Technical courses assessed by technical competencies and mandatory internship. No specific IT course performance results have been published publicly.

blocks.heading: Computing Assessment: Not Yet in Place
blocks.body: No computing-specific assessment instrument exists. SEDUC acknowledges that Mathematics is still a challenge and that specialized teacher training will be needed — the same challenge will apply to computing when the DCRC Complement is homologated.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: The **Currículo da Educação Digital, Midiática e Computacional** (Dec/2025) establishes a transversal approach to the three BNCC Computação axes across all EF and EM disciplines. Educational robotics is active in selected schools as a practical complement. The curriculum was built via public consultation (Sept/2025) in partnership with UNDIME/MS, involving teachers, principals, and students.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, and problem-solving; assemble and program robots applying Mathematics and Natural Sciences concepts.
Educational robotics in selected schools; plugged and unplugged activities integrated into regular disciplines.
Robotics kits, Scratch, unplugged activities, computer labs.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how devices, networks, AI, and cloud computing work; exercise digital rights and develop digital authorship.
Pedagogical use of devices integrated into regular classes; interdisciplinary digital production.
School devices, Google Workspace, educational digital platforms.
Digital Culture & Media Ethics
Develop critical analysis of digital content; combat misinformation; understand LGPD and digital inclusion in the Mato Grosso do Sul context.
Media audit integrated into History, Geography, and Portuguese; debates on AI and digital ethics.
Fact-checking tools, social media platforms, audio and video editors.

items.label: Currículo da Educação Digital, Midiática e Computacional da REE/MS (Dec/2025)
items.note: Complete document with the three BNCC Computação axes mapped for transversality, built via public consultation with UNDIME/MS.

Currículo de Referência REE/MS — Ensino Médio (2021)
Consulta Pública — Currículo Digital (Sept/2025)
items.note: Open form involving teachers, principals, and students.

blocks.heading: Educational Robotics
blocks.body: Active in selected schools with assembly and programming kits. In November 2025, SED expanded robotics training to more teachers, with practical projects such as cleaning robots and other prototypes developed by students.

blocks.heading: Full-Time Schools (Tempo Integral)
blocks.body: **213 of 352 schools** operate in full-time mode, with an extended daily schedule that facilitates the incorporation of technology projects into school routines.

blocks.heading: Favorable Connectivity Context
blocks.body: 95% of households in Mato Grosso do Sul have internet access (PNAD 2024, cited in the Digital Curriculum), signaling a favorable context for digital implementation. The state network has not published specific Wi-Fi coverage data by school.

blocks.heading: Devices
blocks.body: No large-scale individual device purchase program for students. Schools operate with computer labs and personal devices with guided pedagogical use.

blocks.heading: SAEMS
blocks.body: Annual assessment covering Portuguese, Mathematics, and Written Production in Grade 2, 5, and 9 EF and HS Year 3. In 2025, contextual questionnaires for Grade 5 students and teachers were introduced (school climate, infrastructure, and pedagogical practices). Digital competencies are not directly assessed. Public results by school, municipality, and CRE.

blocks.heading: IDAMS and IQE-MS
blocks.body: Indices derived from SAEMS — IDAMS (Learning Development) and IQE-MS (Education Quality by municipality). Both publicly available via portal.

blocks.heading: No Computing-Specific Assessment
blocks.body: No standardized instrument exists for computing competencies. The Digital Curriculum calls for assessment integrated into regular disciplines. The SAEMS contextual questionnaires collect teacher perceptions on infrastructure and practices, but with no specific focus on computing.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: **Computational Thinking** is a mandatory component in 8th and 9th grade EF (2 classes/week). In High School, **Robotics** is part of the Parte Diversificada (1 class/week, 3 modules of 42 classes across the 3 HS years), and **Digital Education** became a mandatory FGB component from 2026. **Programming** via the Escola Digital platform reaches ~500,000 HS students. Robótica Paraná Kids is expanding to Grade 4–5 municipal schools via the Educa Juntos program.

rows: Computational Thinking & Robotics

Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, and programming; assemble and program robots focused on automation, IoT, and home automation; learn block and text programming progressively (intro, advanced programming, robotics applied to projects).
Mandatory component in 8th and 9th EF (2h/week); Robotics in HS with 3 modules of 42 classes per year; practical projects with physical kits and digital platform.
Robotics kits (18,300+ delivered since 2022), Chromebooks, Escola Digital (programming platform), Scratch, Blockly.
Digital World & AI
Develop digital literacy and critical use of technologies; understand and use AI tools to support learning; develop digital authorship in different languages.
Matemática Paraná with Khanmigo (Socratic AI, 500k students); Redação Paraná with AI correction (6 million essays in 2025); pedagogical use of Chromebooks integrated into disciplines.
Escola Digital, Khanmigo (Khan Academy AI), Redação Paraná (AI), Chromebooks (42,195 new in 2025), Inglês Paraná.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze the impact of technologies; develop digital citizenship, ethical use of media, and online safety; understand responsible AI use.
Digital Education as mandatory FGB component in HS (2026); interdisciplinary discussions in integrative projects; transversal approach in other EF years.
Escola Digital, media production tools (video, podcast), digital research platforms.

items.label: Referencial Curricular do Paraná — Interactive Portal (EF and EM)
Instrução Normativa Conjunta nº 001/2025 — Digital Education and Computing
Matrizes Curriculares EM 2026 (IN Conjunta 015/2025) — Digital Education as mandatory FGB
Deliberação CEE/PR — Educação Digital e Computação (public consultation)
Escola Digital — Teacher Portal
items.note: Programming, robotics, platforms, and pedagogical materials.

blocks.heading: Largest State Tech Investment in Brazil (2025)
blocks.body: In 2025: 42,195 Chromebooks, 16,160 desktops, 25,040 tablets, 57,741 headsets, 1,476 interactive screens, 900 printers, and 647 projectors — totaling R$ 187 million for 840,000 students in 1,903 schools. In 2023: 40,680 computers, 18,320 Chromebooks, and 18,300 robotics kits (R$ 200 million). In 2025: 32,000 additional Chromebooks distributed to 1,000+ schools.

blocks.heading: Robótica Paraná
blocks.body: Program launched in 2021, now with **160,000+ students** with access to Robotics classes in the state network. Each school receives kits for 3 modules of 42 classes across HS: introductory robotics (Year 1), advanced programming (Year 2), robotics applied to automation and IoT projects (Year 3).

blocks.heading: Robótica Paraná Kids (2025–2026)
blocks.body: Expansion of robotics to Grade 4–5 municipal schools (EF Early Years) via Programa Educa Juntos, with voluntary municipal adhesion. Includes continuing teacher training and state-funded pedagogical materials.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: **22,500 Wi-Fi access points** installed across all state schools since 2021. In 2025, connectivity expanded to 150 new rural schools via federal program. Seed-PR is a national reference for school connectivity infrastructure.

blocks.heading: AI at Scale
blocks.body: **Khanmigo** (Matemática Paraná) reached 93.77% of HS students Jan–Sept/2025 with average 31 interactions/student and 4.8 million records. **Redação Paraná** generated 6 million essays in 2025 with average 7.7 texts/student.

blocks.heading: SAEP
blocks.body: Annual state assessment covering Portuguese and Mathematics in Grade 5 and 9 EF and HS Year 3. Public results by school via CAEd. Digital competencies are not directly assessed — Computational Thinking and Robotics have no specific external assessment instrument.

blocks.heading: Brazil's Top IDEB Across All Three Stages
blocks.body: Paraná is the state with the best IDEB in Brazil across all three stages (Early Years, Final Years, and HS) — a national quality reference. Public results via INEP.

blocks.heading: Platform-Based Internal Assessment
blocks.body: Escola Digital, Khanmigo, and Redação Paraná generate real-time usage and performance data accessible to principals and teachers. Seed-PR uses this data to monitor progress and adjust pedagogical strategies across its 32 regional education offices.

blocks.heading: Robotics & Computational Thinking Assessment
blocks.body: No standardized external instrument. Assessment is project-based and procedural within each school. The Robotics content is structured in 3 modules with clear progression, but without a published rubric or external results.

blocks.heading: Integration Gap
blocks.body: Teachers report high satisfaction with available digital tools. The main challenge identified is the effective integration of tools into everyday pedagogical practices beyond the specific computing disciplines.

blocks.heading: No Published Computing Curriculum
blocks.body: There is no standalone computing subject and no state-specific complement to BNCC Computação published as of April 2026. The components below reflect what is declared in Portaria nº 1.133/2026 and the transversal approach being incorporated into regular disciplines.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, problem-solving, and algorithms integrated into regular disciplines; investigative practices in EsCriPITEs.
Plugged and unplugged activities in regular disciplines; experimentation workshops in EsCriPITEs; transdisciplinary approach guided by the Guia de Abordagem Transdisciplinar (GAT).
EsCriPITEs, unplugged activities, available computer labs.
Digital World
Understand the use of devices and networks in daily life; develop digital authorship skills integrated into disciplines.
Pedagogical use of available school devices; exploration of SAGEAL as a digital management tool for school principals.
Available school devices, SAGEAL (digital school management system).
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze the impact of technologies; develop digital citizenship with Alagoana identity — connecting indigenous, quilombola, and Afro-Brazilian culture to the digital context.
Debates integrated into History, Portuguese Language, and Humanities; anti-racist approach and territorial identity valorization in the digital context.
Available digital resources, cultural content production platforms.

blocks.heading: Limited Public Access to Curriculum Documents
blocks.body: The ReCAL is referenced on the Escola@Web portal but direct open PDF links are not publicly available. Curricular matrices and pedagogical notebooks are concentrated on Escola@Web, which is primarily for internal network access. The Portaria nº 1.133/2026 is available in the Official Gazette.

items.label: SEDUC-AL — ReCAL (search 'Referencial Curricular')
items.note: Referenced but without a direct open PDF link.

items.label: Escola@Web — Pedagogical Portal (internal network access)
items.note: Curricular matrices, pedagogical notebooks, guides, and teaching materials for the state network.

items.label: Portaria nº 1.133/2026 — 2026 School Year Guidelines
items.note: Establishes BNCC Computação as a transversal pillar. Available in the Official Gazette of Alagoas (Jan/2026).

blocks.heading: EsCriPITEs
blocks.body: Physical innovation spaces installed in selected state schools, with scheduling for recordings, training sessions, and experimentation workshops. They function as teacher training hubs and investigative practice spaces for students. The exact number of units and network coverage have not been officially published.

blocks.heading: One of Brazil's Lowest School Connectivity Rates
blocks.body: According to Censo Escolar 2024, only ~72% of Alagoas public schools have internet access, with adequate speed in an even smaller proportion. The federal Escolas Conectadas program plans connectivity expansion in the state, but no timeline has been published by SEDUC-AL.

blocks.heading: Devices
blocks.body: The state network has not made a large-scale device purchase for students. Schools operate with computer labs with equipment of varying ages. The Moderniza Seduc program focuses on furniture and basic equipment for full-time schools.

blocks.heading: SAGEAL
blocks.body: The **Sistema de Gestão Escolar de Alagoas** requires daily mandatory updates on attendance, enrollment, and school meals. It represents the main digital management infrastructure for the network, but has no direct pedagogical function.

blocks.heading: SAVEAL
blocks.body: Annual census-based assessment covering Portuguese and Mathematics in Grade 2, 5, and 9 EF and HS Year 3, covering state and municipal networks (102 municipalities by adhesion). In 2023, evaluated ~148,000 students. Grade 2 results compose the IQEAL, which determines Educational ICMS distribution to municipalities via Programa Escola 10. Digital competencies are not assessed.

blocks.heading: One of Brazil's Lowest IDEB Scores
blocks.body: Alagoas scored below 4.0 in the HS IDEB 2023, placing the state among the greatest learning challenges in the country. The historical pattern of educational policy discontinuity — including the ReCAL's 'weakened implementation due to management discontinuity' noted by the Observatório Movimento pela Base (2021) — compounds the challenge of implementing computing education.

blocks.heading: No Computing Assessment
blocks.body: Computing has no assessment instrument, rubric, grade, or associated result. The declared transversality of BNCC Computação has no monitoring mechanism described in public documents.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: The **Caderno Complementar BNCC Computação** (2025) maps the three axes transversally for the entire network from early childhood to high school. In full-time schools, **'Educação, Cultura Digital e Inteligência Artificial'** is a standalone component in Grade 6/7 EF and HS Year 2 from 2026, focused on digital security, data protection, and applied AI.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning, algorithms, and problem-solving; understand prototyping and digital fabrication. In full-time schools: deepen programming and applied computational logic.
Plugged and unplugged activities integrated into disciplines; standalone component in full-time schools; Maker labs with 3D printers in excellence schools.
Tablets (71,000 distributed), computer labs, tech carts (30 notebooks each), 3D printers (Makers).
Digital World & AI

Understand how networks, the internet, cloud computing, and AI work; develop digital authorship skills and critical use of AI tools. In full-time schools: specific AI component in Grade 6/7 EF and HS Year 2.
Standalone 'Educação, Cultura Digital e IA' component in full-time schools; pedagogical use of tablets and labs; AVA Escolar for content production and curation by teachers.
Tablets, AVA Escolar, modernized computer labs, fiber optic in 100% of schools.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Develop digital citizenship, ethical and safe technology use; combat misinformation; understand LGPD and data protection; apply ECA Digital (Lei nº 15.211/2025).
Debates integrated into Portuguese, History, and Humanities; 'Ser Cidadão' program for digital ethics and coexistence; ECA Digital protection protocols.
Secure digital platforms, fact-checking tools, Ser Cidadão program.

blocks.heading: 100% Fiber Optic Connectivity (Censo Escolar 2026)
blocks.body: All 319 state schools are connected via fiber optic with managed secure Wi-Fi, confirmed by the 2026 School Census — one of the few Brazilian states to reach this milestone. Connectivity organized across three axes: broadband internet, secure Wi-Fi with attack protection, and device delivery.

blocks.heading: 71,000 Tablets for HS Students (R$ 57 million)
blocks.body: Distribution launched in 2025 for all high school students in the state network. By Jan/2026, 60,000+ devices delivered to 135 schools. Target: completion in 2026.

blocks.heading: Tech Carts
blocks.body: 56 schools without a physical computer lab received **carts with 30 notebooks each**, ensuring technology access in any classroom.

blocks.heading: Maker Labs
blocks.body: Excellence schools receive labs with **3D printers**, integrated into Science, robotics, and technology projects. In Jan/2026, robot presentations were a highlight of the annual Jornada Pedagógica.

blocks.heading: Full-Time Schools
blocks.body: **146 schools (46% of network)** in 7–9h daily schedules, with 'Digital Education' as a mandatory curricular component and 'Educação, Cultura Digital e IA' as a standalone component from 2026 in Grade 6/7 EF and HS Year 2.

blocks.heading: SAESE
blocks.body: Annual assessment covering Portuguese and Mathematics, with public results by school. Full-time schools achieved **97.4% approval rate in 2024**, with consistent ENEM/SISU growth. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: SE participates in the national SAEB. Full-time schools show consistent improvement in SAEB and ENEM indicators. Public results via INEP and QEdu.

blocks.heading: Assessment in Full-Time Schools
blocks.body: The 'Educação, Cultura Digital e IA' component is assessed procedurally by teachers, integrated into the full-time school curricular matrix. No standardized public instrument exists.

blocks.heading: Teacher Development: International Investment
blocks.body: The 'Sergipe no Mundo' program — which sends teachers abroad — now includes a Technology axis with destinations China, Singapore, and Silicon Valley. ~300 teachers completed technology and AI training by Jan/2026. Teachers from the South DRE reported 2,000+ students participating in digital media and computer maintenance courses in 2025.

blocks.heading: UNESCO Award Winner 2025
blocks.body: Piauí was awarded the King Hamad Bin Isa Al-Khalifa Prize by UNESCO in 2025 for its AI in Education initiative — recognizing the state's policy as a global reference. Piauí ranked 2nd nationally in the 2025 AI Olympiad (ONIA), with 1,010 students in the 3rd phase, and had students at the International AI Olympiad (IOAI 2025) in Beijing.

rows: AI & Computational Thinking
Understand AI fundamentals, machine learning, algorithms, and automation; develop AI projects applied to real community problems (e.g., apps for farmers). AWS Cloud Computing certification.
Mandatory subject with interdisciplinary practical projects (AI + Biology + Projeto de Vida); flipped classroom methodology; robotics in 100 schools since 2025.
Google platform (AI subject), Canal Educação (Seduc-PI), AWS (Cloud Computing), TRON robotics kits, Microsoft Teams (teacher training).
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand networks, cloud computing, digital security, and IoT; develop technical skills for the job market via Seduc Tec courses.
Technical courses in Systems Development, Game Programming, and Digital Marketing (Seduc Tec); integration between AI subject and knowledge areas.
Canal Educação, AWS platform, Google for Education.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Critically analyze AI's impact on society; understand algorithmic ethics, LGPD, and responsible data use; develop digital citizenship.
Integrated into the AI subject and Projeto de Vida discussions; Computing Curriculum transversal in EF; Digital and Media Education Complementary Curriculum.
Canal Educação, Google platform, Digital and Media Curriculum materials.

blocks.heading: All HS Schools Full-Time (CETIs)
blocks.body: All state high schools operate as CETIs (Centros Estaduais de Tempo Integral), which made it possible to include the AI subject in the schedule without reducing other disciplines' hours.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: 74.9% school connectivity across the state's ~3,699 public schools (including municipal) — above the national average of 59.65% (Programa Escolas Conectadas, 2025). Providing stable internet to remote areas in the Sertão and southern region remains the main bottleneck.

blocks.heading: Robótica em 100 Escolas (2025)
blocks.body: Partnership with **TRON** for robotics kit supply and training of 100 teachers, benefiting ~15,000 students. Expansion planned.

blocks.heading: Digital Platforms
blocks.body: Canal Educação (Seduc-PI's own platform), Google partnership (AI subject platform), AWS (Cloud Computing module with certification), Microsoft Teams (teacher training).

blocks.heading: Seduc Tec
blocks.body: Vocational education program with technical courses in **Systems Development**, **Game Programming**, and **Digital Marketing**, integrated into HS with AWS certification.

blocks.heading: SAEPI
blocks.body: Annual assessment in Portuguese and Mathematics. AI and computing competencies are not directly assessed. Public results via CAEd.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: PI participates in the national SAEB. Public results via INEP.

blocks.heading: Olympiad Performance as Impact Indicator
blocks.body: Piauí placed 2nd nationally in the 2025 National AI Olympiad (ONIA) with 1,010 students in the 3rd phase. Students participated in the International AI Olympiad (IOAI 2025) in Beijing. These results serve as the main public effectiveness indicator for the program.

blocks.heading: Teacher Certification
blocks.body: The 800 teachers trained in 2024 received certification from Unipampa after a 60-hour program — creating a public qualification record. The hybrid flipped classroom methodology via Canal Educação and Microsoft Teams was documented and evaluated.

blocks.heading: Scaling Challenge
blocks.body: Porvir (2025) identified 'maintaining quality in training new teachers and ensuring infrastructure in remote regions' as the central challenge. Non-STEM teachers (Biology, Physics) report that 60 hours of training is insufficient to cover the most technical aspects of the AI subject in depth.

blocks.heading: No Published Computing Curriculum
blocks.body: There is no standalone computing subject and no state-specific BNCC Computação complement published as of April 2026. The components below reflect BNCC national expectations and the isolated initiatives identified — Educa STEAM and NIEH — without formal state curricular systematization.

rows: Computational Thinking
Develop logical reasoning and problem-solving via STEAM; introduction to digital games and science labs integrated into regular disciplines.
Educa STEAM program: use of educational technologies, digital games, and science labs; NIEH as teacher training and hybrid content production space.
Computer labs (via Computadores para Inclusão), NIEH, digital educational games.
Digital World & Infrastructure
Understand how devices, networks, and the internet work; develop digital authorship skills with the distributed tablets.
Pedagogical use of tablets distributed via Tô Conectado; data chip access for out-of-school use; computer labs in Centros Educa Mais.
Tablets (250,000 in distribution), teacher notebooks, data chips, computer labs.
Digital Culture & Ethics
Develop digital citizenship and critical use of technologies; integrate digital competencies into regular disciplines.
Transversal approach in regular disciplines; CEDIP (Ciclo de Escuta e Diálogos Pedagógicos) as a reflection space on pedagogical practices with technology.
Tablets, digital educational platforms, NIEH.

blocks.heading: No Open Computing Materials Published
blocks.body: No computing curriculum, skill maps, or pedagogical materials have been published by the state network. The Educa STEAM program and NIEH are cited in institutional press releases without public content documentation. The capital's municipal network (São Luís) constituted a BNCC Computação working group in May 2025 — indicating the state network has not yet done so formally.

blocks.heading: Tô Conectado: 250,000 Devices (R$ 275 million)
blocks.body: Distribution of ~250,000 tablets with internet access for HS students and notebooks for all state network teachers. Distribution completed in São Luís and ongoing in the interior in 2025–2026. Data chip partnership with the Ministry of Communications for out-of-school access.

blocks.heading: NIEH (Núcleo de Inovação e Educação Híbrida)
blocks.body: Inaugurated in 2025, functions as a studio for hybrid educational content production, teacher training, and innovative methodology development. No public documentation of content or school coverage.

blocks.heading: Educa STEAM
blocks.body: Program encouraging use of educational technologies, digital games, and science labs in state schools, with **8,000+ participants** in the CEDIP (Ciclo de Escuta e Diálogos Pedagógicos) in 2025.

blocks.heading: Connectivity Gap
blocks.body: 92% of MA public schools have internet access, but only 22.9% were monitored with adequate speed for pedagogical use (Estratégia Nacional de Escolas Conectadas, 2023). The federal government plans to universalize connectivity across the state's 10,417 public schools by 2026, with R$ 8.8 billion federal investment.

blocks.heading: Infrastructure Renovation
blocks.body: 700 schools renovated or restructured in 2025, including Centros Educa Mais, military colleges, and indigenous schools. Programa Computadores para Inclusão (federal) delivering computer labs to Centros Educa Mais in São Luís and Grande Ilha.

blocks.heading: SAEMA
blocks.body: Annual assessment in Portuguese and Mathematics for the state and municipal networks (by adhesion). In 2025, MA achieved 60% in the ICA 2024 (Índice Criança Alfabetizada) — 10th nationally and 4th in the Northeast. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: MA participates in the national SAEB. Public results via INEP.

blocks.heading: No Computing Assessment
blocks.body: Computing has no assessment instrument, rubric, grade, or associated result. Without a formal curriculum, there is no monitoring mechanism for digital competency implementation. The Prêmio Escola Destaque awards 70 schools for literacy performance — no technology criterion exists.

blocks.heading: No Published Computing Curriculum
blocks.body: No standalone computing subject or BNCC Computação complement has been published by SEE-PB as of April 2026. The components below reflect BNCC national expectations, the HS Formative Itinerary catalog, and identified initiatives — without formal state curricular systematization.

blocks.heading: Air Conditioning for 452 Schools (target Dec/2026)
blocks.body: Task force during the 2024/2025 school recess to install air conditioning and upgrade electrical networks across 452 schools — considered a priority for improving conditions for electronic device use in classrooms.

blocks.heading: Full-Time Schools
blocks.body: **Half of 597 state HS schools** operate in full-time mode (35h or 45h per week) — one of the highest proportions in the Northeast. Full-time schools have more favorable structural conditions for incorporating technology.

blocks.heading: International Quantum Computing Center (Dec/2025)
blocks.body: A use agreement was signed between the State Government and the Municipality of João Pessoa to install a quantum computing hub at the Estação das Artes Luciano Agra. This is a science and technology initiative — direct curricular impact on the state network has not yet been publicly documented.

blocks.heading: ParaíbaTEC
blocks.body: Vocational and technology education program linked to PRONATEC, with technical IT courses integrated into HS in approximately one-third of state schools.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: No specific published data on Wi-Fi coverage for the state network. PbEduca requires Google login, suggesting schools have minimum internet access for portal use.

blocks.heading: SAEPE
blocks.body: Annual assessment in Portuguese and Mathematics for state and municipal networks. Results via CAEd portal. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: PB participates in the national SAEB. Public results via INEP.

blocks.heading: No Computing Assessment
blocks.body: Computing has no assessment instrument, rubric, grade, or associated result. Without a formal computing curriculum, there is no monitoring mechanism for digital competency implementation.

blocks.heading: Programa Educação Cidadã Integral (Lei nº 13.533/2024)
blocks.body: Structural program organizing full-time schools and diversified activities. Includes citizenship formation, but without specific computing focus documented.

blocks.heading: Conexão Mundo
blocks.body: Exchange and language program connecting students to international experiences — a sign of internationalization, but without direct relation to curricular computing.

blocks.heading: Context
blocks.body: The **DCEC/RN** (Mar/2026) maps the three BNCC Computação axes for all Basic Education, with transversal implementation in the state network and an option for a dedicated subject in municipal networks. The Innovation Hubs deepen practice with maker labs, robotics, and audiovisual production. COINTE coordinates implementation via 16 regional NATEs.

blocks.heading: 100% State Schools Connected (Geração Conectada)
blocks.body: The Geração Conectada program guaranteed broadband internet in all state schools. In 2025, 80.6% of public schools statewide (state + municipal) achieved adequate quality connectivity (ENEC/MEC), with 2,346 schools receiving PDDE-PIEC resources (R$ 6.8 million).

blocks.heading: Devices
blocks.body: **2,300 notebooks** for teachers and **2,800 Chromebooks** distributed to state schools via Geração Conectada.

blocks.heading: 4 Innovation Hubs — 1st in Brazil (Apr/2024)
blocks.body: Equipped with 3D printers, laser cutters, robotics kits, woodworking, electronics, and prototyping materials. Units: Natal (Apr/2024 — first in Brazil), Pau dos Ferros (Jul/2025), Caicó (Nov/2025), Mossoró (planned 2026).

blocks.heading: RIEH (Rede de Inovação para Educação Híbrida)
blocks.body: Three RIEH units implemented in RN in partnership with the Federal Government, strengthening methodologies that integrate in-person and digital learning.

blocks.heading: COINTE & NATEs
blocks.body: **COINTE** (Coordenadoria de Inovação e Tecnologias Educacionais), created in 2024, is responsible for articulating the digital education policy — continuing training, infrastructure, and pedagogical materials — via **NATEs** (Núcleos de Apoio em Tecnologia Educacional) across 16 Regional Directorates.

blocks.heading: Full-Time Schools
blocks.body: 15,232 new full-time enrollments funded by FUNDEB Oct/2025–Jan/2026 (R$ 60.2 million federal). HS dropout rate fell from 10.2% (2023) to 7.7% (2024).

blocks.heading: SAEPE-RN
blocks.body: Annual assessment in Portuguese and Mathematics for the state network. Public results via CAEd portal. Digital competencies are not directly assessed.

blocks.heading: SAEB
blocks.body: RN participates in the national SAEB. Pé-de-Meia benefited 89,800 students in 2025 (69.7% of public network students). Public results via INEP.

blocks.heading: Computing Assessment
blocks.body: The DCEC/RN (Mar/2026) calls for procedural and formative assessment integrated into regular disciplines. No specific external standardized instrument for computing.

blocks.heading: Participatory Process as Structural Feedback
blocks.body: The DCEC/RN was built via public consultation (Sept–Oct/2025) involving educators, principals, students, researchers, and civil society — making the construction process itself a form of structured feedback. Secretary Socorro Batista stated: 'We are building a collective document.' COINTE is the dedicated institutional body for monitoring digital education implementation via 16 regional NATEs.

blocks.text: Amapá has no standalone computing discipline. Computing is approached transversally through digital tools embedded in existing subjects, aligned with the Computational Thinking and Digital Culture competencies described in the BNCC. There is no published state curriculum supplement (DRC) for computing at this time.
No dedicated computing subject exists. Content is teacher-driven and ad hoc across subjects.

blocks.text: No state-authored computing curriculum or teacher guide has been published. Materials found are national (BNCC-aligned) or from NGOs, not Amapá-specific.

The state secretariat (SEED-AP) operates a portal at seedap.ap.gov.br and regional Nuclei of Technological Education (NTE) offices. The NTEs provide teacher training in basic digital tools (Google Workspace, educational software) but no dedicated CS curriculum support.
Connectivity is a major barrier: many interior schools operate with intermittent or no internet access. Most schools have a teacher's computer or projector at best.

The state NTE (Núcleo de Tecnologia Educacional) network offers teacher training workshops on digital tools. The Bolsa Monitoria program allows university students to support digital inclusion activities in public schools, providing some indirect computing exposure to students.
Device availability is concentrated in capital Macapá schools. Interior municipalities (e.g., Oiapoque, Laranjal do Jari) face infrastructure deficits limiting any technology-based instruction.

Amapá participates in the national SAEB assessment and administers its own PAAP (Programa de Avaliação da Aprendizagem do Amapá) for Portuguese and Mathematics. Neither assessment includes computing or digital literacy components.
No computing-specific assessment exists at the state level. There are no published learning outcomes or benchmarks for digital competencies.

blocks.heading: Not Applicable to General Network
blocks.body: There is no specific mandatory computing subject for non-integral schools. The components below apply exclusively to full-time schools enrolled in the Technology itinerary under the New High School program.

rows: Technology, Innovation, and Environment
Understanding the relationship between technological development, automation (AI), and sustainability (Circular Economy)
Project-Based Learning (PBL): Creating technological solutions for local environmental or social issues
Robotics kits, circuit simulators, modeling software, AI tools
STEAM Culture
Integrating Science, Engineering, and Arts for hands-on prototyping
Maker Culture: Laboratory experimentation and construction of physical and digital prototypes
Maker Labs, design tools, Arduino, recyclable materials

items.label: Applique-se (YouTube)
items.note: General content only — lacks specific Tech/Innovation modules.

blocks.heading: Device Access
blocks.body: The state's **"Conectar Já"** program distributes tablets and Chromebooks, but 1:1 access is largely restricted to full-time (CIEP) schools. In the general stream, device use is occasional and often limited to aging computer labs with ongoing maintenance issues.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
blocks.body: Unstable high-speed internet across several regions creates a dependency on offline or printed materials, significantly hindering the use of cloud-based AI tools and platforms intended for the curriculum.

blocks.heading: Primary Milestone
blocks.body: The **Expotech** (Technology and Innovation Exhibition) serves as the state's main metric, showcasing projects from full-time schools. It functions as a visibility event rather than a systematic assessment instrument.

blocks.heading: Methodology
blocks.body: Practical project-based assessment (robotics, apps, sustainable prototypes) applies to full-time schools. Assessment is nearly non-existent in the general stream, with no standardized digital competency evaluation in place.

blocks.heading: Transparency Gap
blocks.body: No public data on student digital competency exists. Feedback is internal and restricted to authenticated portals, obscuring the real impact on the general student body. Teacher reports highlight high workloads for complex technology projects without technical support, and the exclusion of general stream teachers from the innovation ecosystem.
